name: Statistics
description: Inference (frequentist & Bayesian), regression, hypothesis testing, experimental design, machine learning foundations. The mathematics of learning from data, separating signal from noise, and making decisions under uncertainty. Frequentist statistics controls error rates; Bayesian statistics quantifies posterior uncertainty. Depends on 444 (algebra), 533 (analysis), and 600 (probability as its core engine). Feeds upward into all empirical science domains.
axioms: Bayes' theorem: P(θ|x) = P(x|θ)·P(θ)/P(x) — the complete learning rule
Likelihood principle: all evidence for a parameter is contained in the likelihood function
Law of Large Numbers: estimator consistency as n → ∞
Central Limit Theorem: sampling distribution of mean → normal for large n
Cramér-Rao bound: minimum variance of an unbiased estimator = 1/Fisher information
Neyman-Pearson lemma: most powerful test is a likelihood-ratio test
No free lunch theorem: no single model is universally optimal
reasoning_patterns: hypothesis testing (frequentist: p-values, t-tests; Bayesian: Bayes factors)
power analysis and sample size determination
Bayesian inference with MCMC and variational methods
regularization (ridge, lasso, elastic net) for overfitting control
boundary_conditions: Cannot prove the null hypothesis — only fail to reject it
p-hacking, data dredging, and multiple comparisons invalidate inference
Small samples → wide confidence intervals, high model uncertainty
Observational data cannot establish causation without careful design (or DAGs)
All models are wrong — some are useful (Box)
Statistical significance ≠ practical significance (effect size matters)
